playbooks.title: Money stolen via UPI

steps.title: Note the transaction ID (UTR number)
steps.action: Open PhonePe/GPay/Paytm → History → Find the transaction → Note the 12-15 digit UTR number starting with letters.
steps.why: This is your case reference number for every complaint you file.

steps.title: Call Cyber Crime Helpline 1930 NOW
steps.action: Call 1930 immediately. Say: 'I am reporting a UPI fraud. Transaction UTR: [number]. Amount: ₹[X]. Happened at [time].'
steps.why: They can initiate a hold on the transaction if called within 1-2 hours.

steps.title: Call your bank's 24x7 fraud helpline
steps.action: SBI: 1800 11 2211 | HDFC: 1800 202 6161 | ICICI: 1800 1020 | Axis: 1800 419 5959 | PNB: 1800 180 2222 | BOB: 1800 258 4455 | Kotak: 1800 209 0000 | Yes Bank: 1800 1200 | Union Bank: 1800 22 22 44 | IDFC: 1800 419 4332. Ask them to block your account/UPI and report the fraudulent UTR.
steps.why: Banks can try to stop the fund transfer and protect the rest of your funds.

steps.title: File complaint on cybercrime.gov.in
steps.action: Go to cybercrime.gov.in → Click 'File a Complaint' → 'Financial Fraud' → Fill in UTR, bank details, and scammer details.
steps.why: This is required for legal tracking and potential fund recovery.

steps.title: Visit your bank branch with documents
steps.action: Bring: Aadhaar, PAN, bank statement, transaction screenshot, UTR number, complaint number from 1930. Submit a written complaint.
steps.why: Banks require physical documentation for their investigation process.

steps.title: File FIR at nearest police station
steps.action: Take the cybercrime.gov.in acknowledgment and request a cybercrime FIR specifically. They may redirect you to a cyber cell.
steps.why: FIR is legally required for bank insurance claims in some cases.

whatNotToDo: Don't post on social media yet
Don't contact the scammer again
Don't try to 'recover' money via another service (this is a recovery scam)

playbooks.title: Credit/debit card fraudulent transaction

steps.title: Block the card immediately
steps.action: Open your mobile banking app, navigate to 'Cards' section, and select 'Block/Hotlist Card'. Alternatively, call your bank's toll-free number.
steps.why: Prevents any further unauthorized transactions.

steps.title: Call bank helpline to report fraud
steps.action: SBI: 1800 11 2211 | HDFC: 1800 202 6161 | ICICI: 1800 1020. Report the exact transaction amount and time.
steps.why: Banks must register the fraud complaint to initiate a chargeback.

steps.title: Note down the transaction details
steps.action: Record the amount, merchant name (if visible), and the reference number from the SMS/email alert.
steps.why: Needed for filing the cybercrime complaint.

steps.title: File complaint on cybercrime.gov.in
steps.action: Register the fraud under 'Financial Fraud' on the national portal.
steps.why: Official record required by banks for dispute resolution.

whatNotToDo: Don't wait to see if the money comes back
Don't share the card details with anyone calling to 'help'

playbooks.title: Online banking login compromised

steps.title: Call customer care to freeze account
steps.action: Call your bank's emergency helpline immediately and request a complete freeze on your net banking and account.
steps.why: Stops the attacker from transferring funds.

steps.title: Change passwords if still accessible
steps.action: If you can still log in, change your net banking password and UPI PIN immediately.
steps.why: Locks out the attacker if they don't have secondary authentication.

steps.title: Review recent transactions
steps.action: Check your account statement for any unauthorized payees added or funds transferred.
steps.why: To report exact losses if any occurred.

steps.title: Scan device for malware
steps.action: Run a full scan on your phone/computer using a reputed antivirus.
steps.why: The compromise might be due to a keylogger or spyware.

whatNotToDo: Don't use the same password on other accounts
Don't ignore the breach even if no money is lost yet

playbooks.title: Loan taken in my name (identity theft)

steps.title: Check your CIBIL/Credit Report
steps.action: Download your free credit report from CIBIL, Experian, or CRIF High Mark to identify the fraudulent loan account.
steps.why: Identifies which bank/NBFC issued the fake loan.

steps.title: Contact the loan provider
steps.action: Contact the grievance officer of the bank/app that issued the loan. State it is a case of identity theft.
steps.why: They must flag the account as disputed.

steps.title: File an FIR for Identity Theft
steps.action: Visit the police station and file an FIR under IT Act Section 66C (Identity Theft).
steps.why: Financial institutions require an FIR to clear your name.

steps.title: Raise a dispute with Credit Bureaus
steps.action: Submit a dispute on the CIBIL website attaching the FIR and communication with the lender.
steps.why: To get the fraudulent loan removed from your credit history.

whatNotToDo: Don't pay the EMI of the fraudulent loan out of fear
Don't ignore collection calls (inform them of the fraud)

playbooks.title: Gmail/Google account hacked

steps.title: Go to Google Account Recovery
steps.action: Visit accounts.google.com/signin/recovery and enter your email.
steps.why: Google's official process to reclaim accounts.

steps.title: Use alternate recovery methods
steps.action: If the password is changed, use 'Try another way' to get an OTP on your recovery email or phone number.
steps.why: Bypasses the changed password.

steps.title: Force sign-out all devices
steps.action: Once logged in, go to Security → Your devices → Manage all devices → Sign out of unfamiliar ones.
steps.why: Kicks the hacker out of your account immediately.

steps.title: Check forwarding rules
steps.action: Open Gmail settings → Forwarding and POP/IMAP. Remove any unknown forwarding addresses.
steps.why: Hackers set this up to steal your future emails/OTPs.

steps.title: Enable 2-Step Verification
steps.action: Go to Security → 2-Step Verification and turn it on.
steps.why: Prevents future hacks even if the password is stolen.

whatNotToDo: Don't trust emails claiming to be Google Support asking for your password
Don't delay—hacked emails lead to hacked bank accounts

playbooks.title: Instagram account hacked

steps.title: Check email for changes
steps.action: Look for an email from security@mail.instagram.com saying your email was changed. Click 'Secure my account' if you find it.
steps.why: Reverses the hacker's email change instantly.

steps.title: Request a login link
steps.action: On the login screen, tap 'Get help logging in' (Android) or 'Forgot password' (iPhone). Enter your username and choose your phone number.
steps.why: Sends a secure link to your original device/number.

steps.title: Submit a video selfie
steps.action: If standard recovery fails, tap 'Need more help?' → choose 'I can't access this email/phone'. Follow prompts to submit a video selfie.
steps.why: Instagram uses this to verify you are the real owner.

steps.title: Warn your contacts
steps.action: Tell friends on WhatsApp/other apps that your account is hacked and they shouldn't send money or click links from it.
steps.why: Hackers often run crypto or emergency scams on your followers.

whatNotToDo: Don't pay 'recovery hackers' on Twitter/Google—they are scams
Don't create a new account until you've tried recovering this one

playbooks.title: Facebook account hacked

steps.title: Go to the compromised page
steps.action: Visit facebook.com/hacked on a browser.
steps.why: This is Facebook's dedicated portal for compromised accounts.

steps.title: Identify your account
steps.action: Search by your phone number, email, or profile name.
steps.why: Locates the account even if the hacker changed the name.

steps.title: Use old passwords
steps.action: Enter a previous password you remember. Facebook allows recovery if it recognizes an old password.
steps.why: Proves your identity prior to the hack.

steps.title: Review linked accounts
steps.action: Check if your Instagram or Meta Ads accounts were also compromised and secure them.
steps.why: Hackers often exploit linked business accounts to run ads.

whatNotToDo: Don't trust any links sent by friends if their accounts might be hacked too

playbooks.title: WhatsApp account hacked/taken over

steps.title: Re-register your number immediately
steps.action: Open WhatsApp, enter your phone number, and verify with the SMS code you receive.
steps.why: Logging in on your phone automatically logs the hacker out.

steps.title: Wait 7 days if PIN is asked
steps.action: If the hacker set up a 2-Step PIN, you must wait 7 days to reset it. But they are already logged out from step 1.
steps.why: Protects the account from being re-accessed by the hacker.

steps.title: Inform your contacts via SMS/Call
steps.action: Tell family and friends your WhatsApp was compromised and to ignore requests for money.
steps.why: Hackers message contacts asking for urgent UPI transfers.

whatNotToDo: Don't ever share a WhatsApp registration SMS code with anyone
Don't ignore it if you randomly get a WhatsApp registration code

playbooks.title: Email account compromised + 2FA lost

steps.title: Start Account Recovery
steps.action: Go to the provider's recovery page and select 'I don't have my phone' or 'I lost my 2FA device'.
steps.why: Initiates the manual review process.

steps.title: Provide backup codes (if any)
steps.action: Use the 8-digit backup codes you saved when setting up 2FA.
steps.why: This is exactly what they are for.

steps.title: Fill out identity verification forms
steps.action: Answer questions about when you created the account, frequent contacts, and previous passwords.
steps.why: Helps the provider verify you are the real owner.

whatNotToDo: Don't submit multiple recovery requests in a row, it flags you as spam

playbooks.title: Phone stolen (with all banking apps)

steps.title: Block your SIM card immediately
steps.action: Call your telecom provider (Jio, Airtel, Vi) from another phone and block the SIM.
steps.why: Prevents the thief from receiving OTPs to reset your bank passwords.

steps.title: Remotely wipe the device
steps.action: Go to android.com/find (Android) or icloud.com/find (iPhone) and select 'Erase Device'.
steps.why: Deletes all your personal data, photos, and apps.

steps.title: De-link UPI and Bank accounts
steps.action: Call your bank and ask them to disable UPI and mobile banking for your account.
steps.why: Extra safety in case the SIM block was delayed.

steps.title: Block the phone via CEIR
steps.action: File a police complaint, then visit ceir.gov.in to block the IMEI number of the phone.
steps.why: Makes the phone completely useless across all Indian networks.

whatNotToDo: Don't try to track and confront the thief yourself
Don't delay blocking the SIM—it is the key to all your accounts

playbooks.title: Ransomware — files encrypted

steps.title: Disconnect from Network/WiFi
steps.action: Unplug the ethernet cable and turn off WiFi immediately.
steps.why: Stops the ransomware from spreading to other computers on the same network.

steps.title: Do not reboot or pay
steps.action: Leave the computer on. Do not pay the ransom.
steps.why: Paying funds criminals, and there's no guarantee you'll get your files back.

steps.title: Report to CERT-In
steps.action: Report the incident to the Indian Computer Emergency Response Team at cert-in.org.in.
steps.why: Official reporting for severe cyber incidents.

steps.title: Check NoMoreRansom.org
steps.action: Visit nomoreransom.org on a safe device to see if a free decryption tool exists for your specific ransomware.
steps.why: Security researchers release free tools for many ransomware variants.

whatNotToDo: Don't plug in any USB drives or external hard drives
Don't pay the ransom

playbooks.title: Suspected spyware/stalkerware on device

steps.title: Turn on Airplane Mode
steps.action: Enable Airplane mode immediately.
steps.why: Stops the spyware from sending your data to the attacker.

steps.title: Check Device Admins (Android)
steps.action: Go to Settings → Security → Device admin apps. Turn off access for anything suspicious.
steps.why: Spyware often hides as a device administrator.

steps.title: Review Installed Apps
steps.action: Look through your full app list for apps you don't recognize. Delete them.
steps.why: Removes the malicious application.

steps.title: Factory Reset (Safest Option)
steps.action: Backup only photos/contacts, then perform a complete Factory Data Reset.
steps.why: The only guaranteed way to remove deep-rooted spyware.

whatNotToDo: Don't restore apps from a backup after a factory reset, reinstall them manually

playbooks.title: Personal photos/videos leaked online

steps.title: Preserve Evidence
steps.action: Take screenshots of where the photos are posted, including URLs and user profiles.
steps.why: Needed for police and platform reports.

steps.title: Report to the Platform
steps.action: Use the report button on Instagram/Facebook/Twitter and select 'Non-consensual intimate imagery (NCII)'.
steps.why: Platforms prioritize removing this type of content quickly.

steps.title: Submit to StopNCII.org
steps.action: Go to stopncii.org and generate a hash of your images/videos.
steps.why: This global initiative prevents the images from being shared on major social media platforms.

steps.title: File Police Complaint
steps.action: Report on cybercrime.gov.in under 'Crime Against Women/Children' or file an FIR under IT Act Section 66E.
steps.why: Legal action against the perpetrator.

whatNotToDo: Don't interact with trolls or the person who posted it
Don't blame yourself

playbooks.title: Being sextorted (intimate images threat)

steps.title: DO NOT PAY
steps.action: Stop all communication. Do not send money.
steps.why: Paying guarantees they will ask for more. It never stops the extortion.

steps.title: Screenshot Everything
steps.action: Save the chat logs, threats, phone numbers, and payment details requested.
steps.why: Crucial evidence for the police.

steps.title: Deactivate Social Media
steps.action: Temporarily deactivate Facebook/Instagram and tighten privacy settings.
steps.why: Prevents the scammer from finding and messaging your family/friends.

steps.title: Report to Police
steps.action: Call 1930 or file a report on cybercrime.gov.in immediately.
steps.why: Law enforcement can trace the extortionists.

whatNotToDo: DO NOT PAY the ransom
Do not delete the chat history until you have screenshots

playbooks.title: Personal information posted without consent (doxxing)

steps.title: Screenshot the post
steps.action: Capture the post, the user's handle, and the URL.
steps.why: Evidence before it gets deleted.

steps.title: Report to platform
steps.action: Report the post for 'Sharing personal information' or 'Harassment'.
steps.why: Platforms violate terms of service for doxxing.

steps.title: Secure your accounts
steps.action: Ensure all social media accounts are private and 2FA is enabled.
steps.why: Doxxing often leads to targeted hacking attempts.

steps.title: Warn family/employer
steps.action: If your home address or workplace was shared, inform your family and HR department.
steps.why: Safety precaution against physical harassment.

whatNotToDo: Don't engage with the person who doxed you

playbooks.title: Aadhaar number misused / fake SIM issued

steps.title: Check active SIM cards
steps.action: Visit tafcop.sancharsaathi.gov.in, enter your mobile number, and check all numbers registered to your Aadhaar.
steps.why: Identifies if someone took a SIM in your name.

steps.title: Report unauthorized numbers
steps.action: On the TAFCOP portal, select 'This is not my number' for any unrecognized numbers and report them.
steps.why: Telecom dept will disconnect the fake SIMs.

steps.title: Lock Aadhaar Biometrics
steps.action: Download mAadhaar app or go to uidai.gov.in to lock your biometrics.
steps.why: Prevents unauthorized fingerprint/iris authentication.

steps.title: Check Aadhaar Authentication History
steps.action: On the UIDAI portal, view your authentication history for the last 6 months.
steps.why: To see exactly where and when your Aadhaar was misused.

whatNotToDo: Don't ignore unknown numbers listed under your Aadhaar on TAFCOP
